[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Wembley_Stadium_closeup.jpg/330px-Wembley_Stadium_closeup.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wembley_Stadium_closeup.jpg)

The new [Wembley Stadium](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium "Wembley Stadium") was completed in time for the 2006–07 season's [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup") [Final](/wiki/2007_FA_Cup_Final "2007 FA Cup Final").

The **2006–07 season** was the 127th season of competitive [association football in England](/wiki/Association_football_in_England "Association football in England").

## Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2006%E2%80%9307_in_English_football&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Overview")]

* Manchester United regain the league title for the first time in four years, overcoming a stiff challenge from defending champions Chelsea to be crowned Premier League victors for the 9th time in 16 seasons
* The number of divisions at Level 8 of the [English football league system](/wiki/English_football_league_system "English football league system") increased from four to five. Level 9 decreased from fifteen divisions to fourteen.
* [Wembley Stadium](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium "Wembley Stadium") was completed to host the [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup") Final,[[1]](#cite_note-1) however it was not ready for the [national team](/wiki/England_national_football_team "England national football team")'s first three [2008 UEFA European Football Championship](/wiki/2008_UEFA_European_Football_Championship "2008 UEFA European Football Championship") home qualifiers. The three matches were played at [Old Trafford](/wiki/Old_Trafford_(football_ground) "Old Trafford (football ground)") in Manchester.
* [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C.") moved into their new home, the 60,000-capacity [Emirates Stadium](/wiki/Emirates_Stadium "Emirates Stadium").[[2]](#cite_note-2) [Emirates](/wiki/Emirates_(airline) "Emirates (airline)") became the club's shirt sponsor.[[3]](#cite_note-3)
* Following promotion from the [Championship](/wiki/Football_League_Championship "Football League Championship"), [Reading](/wiki/Reading_F.C. "Reading F.C.") played in the [Premiership](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League") and the "top flight" of English football for the first time in their 135-year history and won their first game, coming back from 0–2 down to beat [Middlesbrough](/wiki/Middlesbrough_F.C. "Middlesbrough F.C.") 3–2.[[4]](#cite_note-4) They went on to finish 8th.
* [Accrington Stanley](/wiki/Accrington_Stanley_F.C. "Accrington Stanley F.C.") (the third, present version) played in the [Football League](/wiki/The_Football_League "The Football League") for the first time.[[5]](#cite_note-accrington-5) The previous club of the same name dissolved with massive debts and in 1962 became the first club to leave the Football League mid-season.[[5]](#cite_note-accrington-5)
* [Oxford United](/wiki/Oxford_United_F.C. "Oxford United F.C.") played in the [Conference National](/wiki/Conference_National "Conference National") after being relegated from the Football League.[[6]](#cite_note-6) They are the first former winners of a major trophy to play at this level, having won the [League Cup](/wiki/Football_League_Cup "Football League Cup") in 1986.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Diary of the season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2006%E2%80%9307_in_English_football&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Diary of the season")]

* 22 June 2006 – Fixtures for the Premier League and the Football League are released.[[8]](#cite_note-8)
* 30 June 2006 – [Rupert Lowe](/wiki/Rupert_Lowe "Rupert Lowe") resigns from [Southampton](/wiki/Southampton_F.C. "Southampton F.C."). [Michael Wilde](/wiki/Michael_Wilde "Michael Wilde")'s consortium takes control of the club.[[9]](#cite_note-9)
* 27 July 2006 – [A consortium](/wiki/Drumaville_Consortium "Drumaville Consortium") led by [Niall Quinn](/wiki/Niall_Quinn "Niall Quinn") takes full control of [Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland_A.F.C. "Sunderland A.F.C.") after previously buying out the former chairman [Bob Murray](/wiki/Bob_Murray_(businessman) "Bob Murray (businessman)").[[10]](#cite_note-10)
* 1 August 2006 – [Steve McClaren](/wiki/Steve_McClaren "Steve McClaren") begins his job as [England](/wiki/England_national_football_team "England national football team") manager.[[11]](#cite_note-11)
* 2 August 2006 – [Leeds United](/wiki/Leeds_United_A.F.C. "Leeds United A.F.C.") chairman [Ken Bates](/wiki/Ken_Bates "Ken Bates") reports his former club [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C.") to [The Football Association](/wiki/The_Football_Association "The Football Association"), [Premier League](/wiki/FA_Premier_League "FA Premier League") and [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA") over the alleged 'tapping-up' of three Leeds youth team players.[[12]](#cite_note-12)
* 5 August 2006 – The Football League season begins, with [Luton Town](/wiki/Luton_Town_F.C. "Luton Town F.C.") beating [Leicester City](/wiki/Leicester_City_F.C. "Leicester City F.C.") 2–0 in the first game.[[13]](#cite_note-13)
* 8 August 2006 – The 500,000th goal in the history of English league football ([FA Premier League](/wiki/FA_Premier_League "FA Premier League") and [Football League](/wiki/The_Football_League "The Football League")) was scored by [Huddersfield Town](/wiki/Huddersfield_Town_F.C. "Huddersfield Town F.C.")'s [Gary Taylor-Fletcher](/wiki/Gary_Taylor-Fletcher "Gary Taylor-Fletcher") in their 3–0 victory over [Rotherham United](/wiki/Rotherham_United_F.C. "Rotherham United F.C.").[[14]](#cite_note-14)
* 10 August 2006 – [John Terry](/wiki/John_Terry "John Terry") is appointed as the new captain of England.[[15]](#cite_note-15)
* 12 August 2006 – The [Football Conference](/wiki/Football_Conference "Football Conference") begins, along with most other non-league campaigns.[[16]](#cite_note-16)
* 13 August 2006 – [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C.") take the first silverware of the season, as they beat Chelsea 2–1 in the [FA Community Shield](/wiki/FA_Community_Shield "FA Community Shield") at the [Millennium Stadium](/wiki/Millennium_Stadium "Millennium Stadium") in [Cardiff](/wiki/Cardiff "Cardiff").[[17]](#cite_note-17)
* 19 August 2006 – The Premier League season begins with [Sheffield United](/wiki/Sheffield_United_F.C. "Sheffield United F.C.") and [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C.") drawing 1–1.[[18]](#cite_note-18)
* 23 August 2006 – [Manchester City](/wiki/Manchester_City_F.C. "Manchester City F.C.") defender [Ben Thatcher](/wiki/Ben_Thatcher "Ben Thatcher") elbows [Portsmouth](/wiki/Portsmouth_F.C. "Portsmouth F.C.")'s [Pedro Mendes](/wiki/Pedro_Mendes_(footballer,_born_1979) "Pedro Mendes (footballer, born 1979)") in the face at the [City of Manchester Stadium](/wiki/City_of_Manchester_Stadium "City of Manchester Stadium"), after the two challenged for the same ball. This left Mendes unconscious and needing hospital treatment.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Thatcher was shown a [yellow card](/wiki/Misconduct_(football) "Misconduct (football)") for the incident, but he was later banned by his club and charged with violent misconduct by [The FA](/wiki/The_Football_Association "The Football Association").[[20]](#cite_note-20) [Ole Gunnar Solskjær](/wiki/Ole_Gunnar_Solskj%C3%A6r "Ole Gunnar Solskjær") scores his first competitive goal in more than three years in Manchester United's 3–0 away league win over [Charlton Athletic](/wiki/Charlton_Athletic_F.C. "Charlton Athletic F.C.").[[21]](#cite_note-21)
* 31 August 2006 – [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C.") are fined £40,000 by The FA for breaching doping regulations.[[22]](#cite_note-22)
* 31 August 2006: August ends with last season's runners-up [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") top of the Premiership with three wins out of three. [Portsmouth](/wiki/Portsmouth_F.C. "Portsmouth F.C."), [Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa_F.C. "Aston Villa F.C."), [Everton](/wiki/Everton_F.C. "Everton F.C."), [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C."), [West Ham United](/wiki/West_Ham_United_F.C. "West Ham United F.C.") and [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C.") complete the top seven. [Watford](/wiki/Watford_F.C. "Watford F.C."), [Sheffield United](/wiki/Sheffield_United_F.C. "Sheffield United F.C.") and [Blackburn Rovers](/wiki/Blackburn_Rovers_F.C. "Blackburn Rovers F.C.") prop up the table with one point each from their opening three matches, with [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C.") only above the relegation zone on goal difference. Cardiff City lead the Championship, with Norwich City, Crystal Palace, Birmingham City and Wolverhampton Wanderers all three points behind the Welsh club and Burnley completing the top six, ahead of Plymouth Argyle and West Bromwich Albion on goal difference. Sunderland, despite playing in a lower division, have failed to turn around their poor form from the previous season and stand in the Championship drop zone, sandwiched by Colchester United and Hull City. Nottingham Forest lead the way in League One, with Port Vale second. Bradford, Tranmere, Brentford and Cheltenham Town completing the top six. Rotherham, only bottom after a 10 point deduction for due to entering a Company Voluntary Agreement, Scunthorpe United and Northampton Town sandwiching Millwall,failing to turn around their fortunes after relegation last season. Swindon Town, Walsall and Milton Keynes Dons lie in the automatic promotion places, with Peterborough and Lincoln a point behind. Wycombe and Wrecham complete the play-off places. Macclesfield and Rochdale in the drop zone.
* 4 September 2006 – [Andy Webster](/wiki/Andy_Webster_(footballer,_born_1982) "Andy Webster (footballer, born 1982)")'s controversial move to [Wigan Athletic](/wiki/Wigan_Athletic_F.C. "Wigan Athletic F.C.") is ratified by [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA").[[23]](#cite_note-23)
* 8 September 2006 – [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C.") get permission from Liverpool city council to build a [new 60,000 all-seater stadium](/wiki/Stanley_Park_Stadium "Stanley Park Stadium") in nearby [Stanley Park](/wiki/Stanley_Park,_Liverpool "Stanley Park, Liverpool").[[24]](#cite_note-24)
* 18 September 2006 – [Bryan Robson](/wiki/Bryan_Robson "Bryan Robson") is sacked after less than two years as manager of [West Bromwich Albion](/wiki/West_Bromwich_Albion_F.C. "West Bromwich Albion F.C.").[[25]](#cite_note-25)
* 19 September 2006 – After becoming majority shareholder at the club, [Randy Lerner](/wiki/Randy_Lerner "Randy Lerner") officially becomes chairman of [Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa_F.C. "Aston Villa F.C."), replacing 82-year-old [Doug Ellis](/wiki/Doug_Ellis "Doug Ellis").[[26]](#cite_note-26) A [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") *[Panorama](/wiki/Panorama_(TV_series) "Panorama (TV series)")* programme is aired, [alleging widespread corruption](/wiki/2006_allegations_of_corruption_in_English_football "2006 allegations of corruption in English football") in the English game.[[27]](#cite_note-27)
* 30 September 2006 – September ends with reigning champions Chelsea top of the Premiership. Bolton Wanderers are keeping up their push for a European place, being only two points behind in second place. Manchester United, Portsmouth, Everton, Aston Villa and Arsenal complete the top seven, with Tottenham Hotspur, Watford and Charlton Athletic in the relegation zone. Cardiff have extended their lead in the Championship to five points, ahead of Birmingham. Preston North End, Crystal Palace, Southampton and West Brom stand in the play-off places and Southend United, Hull City and Sheffield Wednesday in the relegation zone. Nottingham Forest continue to lead League One, with a three point cushion over their nearest challenges Yeovil Town and Bristol City, with Yeovil above Bristol City on goal difference. Bradford, Tranmere and Carlisle in the play-off places, with Scunthorpe a point behind, recovering from their slow start. Rotherham, Leyton Orient, Millwall and Blackpool in the bottom four. Walsall lead League Two, with Swindon and Wycombe completing the automatic promotion places. Lincoln, MK Dons, Notts County and Hartlepool complete the top seven. Macclesfield and Boston United in the drop zone.
* 1 October 2006 – [Norwich City](/wiki/Norwich_City_F.C. "Norwich City F.C."), 17th in the Championship, sack manager [Nigel Worthington](/wiki/Nigel_Worthington "Nigel Worthington") after nearly six years in charge.[[28]](#cite_note-28)
* 14 October 2006 – [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C.") goalkeepers, [Petr Čech](/wiki/Petr_%C4%8Cech "Petr Čech") and [Carlo Cudicini](/wiki/Carlo_Cudicini "Carlo Cudicini"), both received head injuries during their match against [Reading](/wiki/Reading_F.C. "Reading F.C.").[[29]](#cite_note-29) Čech underwent surgery for a depressed skull fracture and is expected to be out for six months,[[30]](#cite_note-cechsurgery-30) while Cudicini was treated and released.[[30]](#cite_note-cechsurgery-30)
* 16 October 2006 – [Norwich City](/wiki/Norwich_City_F.C. "Norwich City F.C.") appoint [West Ham United](/wiki/West_Ham_United_F.C. "West Ham United F.C.") assistant manager [Peter Grant](/wiki/Peter_Grant_(footballer,_born_1965) "Peter Grant (footballer, born 1965)") as their new manager.[[31]](#cite_note-31)
* 18 October 2006 – [Hibernian](/wiki/Hibernian_F.C. "Hibernian F.C.")'s [Tony Mowbray](/wiki/Tony_Mowbray "Tony Mowbray") is appointed manager of West Bromwich Albion.[[32]](#cite_note-32)
* 28 October 2006 – [Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa_F.C. "Aston Villa F.C."), the last unbeaten team in any professional English division, lose 3–1 to [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C.") at [Anfield](/wiki/Anfield "Anfield").[[33]](#cite_note-33)
* 30 October 2006 – Manchester United have moved to the top of the Premiership on goal difference; both United and Chelsea have 25 points apiece. Bolton, Portsmouth, Arsenal, Everton and Aston Villa complete the top seven, while Watford, Sheffield United and Charlton Athletic finish the month in the relegation zone. Cardiff remain top of the Championship and Preston have claimed second place, with Burnley, Birmingham, West Brom and Plymouth not far behind. Hull, Barnsley and Southend stand in the bottom three.
* 21 November 2006 – [West Ham United](/wiki/West_Ham_United_F.C. "West Ham United F.C.") accept [Eggert Magnússon](/wiki/Eggert_Magn%C3%BAsson "Eggert Magnússon")'s £85 million takeover bid for the club.[[34]](#cite_note-34)
* 29 November 2006 – [England National Game XI](/wiki/England_National_Game_XI "England National Game XI"), the English semi-professional team, win the inaugural European Challenge Trophy after a 4–1 win over the Netherlands.[[35]](#cite_note-35)
* 30 November 2006 – Manchester United remain top of the Premiership, three points ahead of Chelsea. Portsmouth, having narrowly avoided relegation the previous season, are maintaining their European push although they are 11 points adrift of Chelsea in third. Bolton, Aston Villa, Arsenal and Liverpool complete the top seven, while Sheffield United, Watford and Charlton Athletic remain in the relegation zone. Preston have leaped to the top of the Championship, a point ahead of Cardiff, Birmingham and Derby County. Burnley and Stoke City are also challenging for a play-off spot, while Leeds United, Hull and Southend are battling relegation.
* 5 December 2006 – [Macclesfield Town](/wiki/Macclesfield_Town_F.C. "Macclesfield Town F.C."), the last team without a win in the Football League, beat [Rochdale](/wiki/Rochdale_A.F.C "Rochdale A.F.C") 1–0 at [Moss Rose](/wiki/Moss_Rose "Moss Rose").[[36]](#cite_note-36)
* 20 December 2006 – [Bury](/wiki/Bury_F.C. "Bury F.C.") are thrown out of [this season](/wiki/2006%E2%80%9307_FA_Cup "2006–07 FA Cup")'s [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup") after they fielded an ineligible player, [Stephen Turnbull](/wiki/Stephen_Turnbull_(footballer,_born_1987) "Stephen Turnbull (footballer, born 1987)"), on-loan from [Hartlepool United](/wiki/Hartlepool_United_F.C. "Hartlepool United F.C.") in their 3–1 second round replay win at [Chester City](/wiki/Chester_City_F.C. "Chester City F.C.") on 12 December. Chester will replace Bury in the third round.[[37]](#cite_note-37) [Lord Stevens](/wiki/John_Stevens,_Baron_Stevens_of_Kirkwhelpington "John Stevens, Baron Stevens of Kirkwhelpington") releases the results of his inquiry in the allegation of corruption in the English game, pin-pointing seventeen transfers that he will investigate further.[[38]](#cite_note-38)
* 23 December 2006 – [Doncaster Rovers](/wiki/Doncaster_Rovers_F.C. "Doncaster Rovers F.C.") play their final game at [Belle Vue](/wiki/Belle_Vue_(football) "Belle Vue (football)") against [Nottingham Forest](/wiki/Nottingham_Forest_F.C. "Nottingham Forest F.C."). They move into the 15,000 seat [Keepmoat Stadium](/wiki/Keepmoat_Stadium "Keepmoat Stadium").[[39]](#cite_note-39)
* 30 December 2006 – [Moritz Volz](/wiki/Moritz_Volz "Moritz Volz") of [Fulham](/wiki/Fulham_F.C. "Fulham F.C.") scored the 15,000th goal in the history of the [F.A. Premier League](/wiki/F.A._Premier_League "F.A. Premier League"), opening the scoring in his side's 2–2 draw away at [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C.").[[40]](#cite_note-40)
* 30 December 2006 – Manchester United have increased the gap between them and Chelsea, still in second place, to six points. Bolton, Liverpool, Arsenal, Portsmouth and Everton complete the top seven. Watford see in the New Year being bottom of the Premiership and ten points adrift of safety, with West Ham United and Charlton Athletic also several points behind fellow strugglers Sheffield United, Wigan Athletic and Middlesbrough. Birmingham now lead the Championship, six points ahead of Preston and Derby. Colchester have overcome a slow start to stand in sixth, behind Southampton and West Brom. Hull, Leeds and Southend remain in the Championship drop zone.
* 11 January 2007 – Former [Barrow](/wiki/Barrow_A.F.C. "Barrow A.F.C.") defender [James Cotterill](/wiki/James_Cotterill "James Cotterill") is sentenced to four months in prison for causing [grievous bodily harm](/wiki/Grievous_bodily_harm "Grievous bodily harm") to [Bristol Rovers](/wiki/Bristol_Rovers_F.C. "Bristol Rovers F.C.") player [Sean Rigg](/wiki/Sean_Rigg "Sean Rigg") during an [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup") first round match in November 2006. Rigg suffered a double fracture of the jaw after being punched by Cotterill.[[41]](#cite_note-41)
* 16 January 2007 – In the FA Cup third round replays, third tier [Bristol City](/wiki/Bristol_City_F.C. "Bristol City F.C.") knock out Championship club [Coventry City](/wiki/Coventry_City_F.C. "Coventry City F.C."). Elsewhere, top-flight sides Manchester City and Middlesbrough survive scares against [Football League](/wiki/English_Football_League "English Football League") teams to progress, beating Sheffield Wednesday and Hull City respectively.[[42]](#cite_note-42)
* 25 January 2007 – [Peter Taylor](/wiki/Peter_Taylor_(footballer,_born_1953) "Peter Taylor (footballer, born 1953)") resigns as [England under-21](/wiki/England_national_under-21_football_team "England national under-21 football team") manager, stating that he wants to focus on managing his other team, [Crystal Palace](/wiki/Crystal_Palace_F.C. "Crystal Palace F.C.").[[43]](#cite_note-43)
* 31 January 2007 – Manchester United remain top of the Premiership and look safe to reclaim the title they last won in 2003. Chelsea remain six points behind, with Liverpool, Arsenal, Bolton, Portsmouth and Reading (the latter expected by many to struggle in their first ever top-flight campaign) completing the top seven. The relegation zone remains unchanged from the end of December, but the strugglers are cranking up the pressure on Wigan Athletic. Derby end the month as the leader of the Championship, six points clear of Birmingham (who have two games in hand) and Preston, with West Brom, Southampton and a resurgent Cardiff not far behind. Leeds has swapped places with Southend at the foot of the Championship while Hull have escaped the bottom three at the expense of QPR.
* 1 February. 2007 – [Stuart Pearce](/wiki/Stuart_Pearce "Stuart Pearce") is appointed as Peter Taylor's replacement.[[44]](#cite_note-44)
* 6 February 2007 – [George Gillett](/wiki/George_N._Gillett_Jr. "George N. Gillett Jr.") and [Tom Hicks](/wiki/Tom_Hicks "Tom Hicks") complete their takeover of [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C.").[[45]](#cite_note-45)
* 7 February 2007 – [Milan Mandarić](/wiki/Milan_Mandari%C4%87 "Milan Mandarić") completes his takeover of [Leicester City](/wiki/Leicester_City_F.C. "Leicester City F.C.").[[46]](#cite_note-46)
* 25 February 2007 – [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C.") defeat [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C.") 2–1 in the [Carling Cup final](/wiki/Football_League_Cup_Final_2007 "Football League Cup Final 2007") at the [Millennium Stadium](/wiki/Millennium_Stadium "Millennium Stadium"). The match was marred by a mass brawl between the two sides during the final few minutes of the game, in which three players were sent off.[[47]](#cite_note-47)
* 28 March 2007 – Manchester United now lead Chelsea by nine points, but have played one match more. Liverpool, Arsenal and Bolton are still in the mix for a Champions League place, with sixth-placed Reading and seventh-place Everton's challenge for [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup "UEFA Cup") qualification is being threatened by Portsmouth and Tottenham. Wigan remains six points ahead of eighteenth-placed Charlton, joined in the relegation zone by West Ham and Watford. Birmingham are back on top of the Championship, a point ahead of West Brom and Derby. An improving Sunderland have moved into the top six, ahead of Southampton and Cardiff. The relegation zone is unchanged from the end of January.
* 3 March 2007 – [Stockport County](/wiki/Stockport_County "Stockport County") set a [Football League](/wiki/Football_League "Football League") record of nine consecutive wins without conceding a goal by beating [Swindon Town](/wiki/Swindon_Town "Swindon Town") 3–0 at [Edgeley Park](/wiki/Edgeley_Park "Edgeley Park").[[48]](#cite_note-48) Goalkeeper [Wayne Hennessey](/wiki/Wayne_Hennessey "Wayne Hennessey") keeps the club record nine consecutive clean sheets and is named Player of the Month for February 2007.[[49]](#cite_note-49)
* 17 March 2007 – The new [Wembley Stadium](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium "Wembley Stadium") opens for a special community event.[[50]](#cite_note-50)
* 31 March 2007 – Chelsea have moved to within six points of leaders Manchester United but, with only seven matches left to play, there is little hope of the Londoners retaining the title for a third successive season. Liverpool and Arsenal have put distance between Bolton in the chase for the Champions League, who complete the top seven with Everton and Tottenham. Charlton, West Ham and Watford remain in the relegation zone but have caught up with strugglers Wigan and Sheffield United, now only three points and one point above the relegation zone respectively. Sunderland have finally moved into the automatic promotion spots in the Championship, sandwiched by leaders Derby and Birmingham. Preston, West Brom and Wolves are in the play-off places. Luton Town have slipped to the bottom of the table, behind Southend and Leeds.
* 1 April 2007 – [Doncaster Rovers](/wiki/Doncaster_Rovers_F.C. "Doncaster Rovers F.C.") win the competition for lower-division football league clubs, as they beat [Bristol Rovers](/wiki/Bristol_Rovers_F.C. "Bristol Rovers F.C.") 3–2 after extra time in the [Johnstone's Paint Trophy](/wiki/Johnstone%27s_Paint_Trophy "Johnstone's Paint Trophy") at the [Millennium Stadium](/wiki/Millennium_Stadium "Millennium Stadium") in [Cardiff](/wiki/Cardiff "Cardiff").[[51]](#cite_note-51)
* 7 April 2007 – [Dagenham & Redbridge](/wiki/Dagenham_%26_Redbridge_F.C. "Dagenham & Redbridge F.C.") win the [Conference National](/wiki/Conference_National "Conference National") and promotion to the [Football League](/wiki/Football_League "Football League").[[52]](#cite_note-52)
* 9 April 2007 – [St Albans City](/wiki/St_Albans_City_F.C. "St Albans City F.C.") are relegated from the [Conference National](/wiki/Conference_National "Conference National").[[53]](#cite_note-53) [Brentford](/wiki/Brentford_F.C. "Brentford F.C.") are relegated to League Two.[[54]](#cite_note-54)
* 10 April 2007 – Manchester United beat [Roma](/wiki/A.S._Roma "A.S. Roma") 7–1 in the Champions League quarter-final second leg at [Old Trafford](/wiki/Old_Trafford "Old Trafford"), completing an 8–3 aggregate victory and booking their first semi-final appearance in the competition for five years.[[55]](#cite_note-55)
* 14 April 2007 – [Torquay United](/wiki/Torquay_United_F.C. "Torquay United F.C.") are relegated to the Conference.[[56]](#cite_note-56) [Scunthorpe United](/wiki/Scunthorpe_United_F.C. "Scunthorpe United F.C.") are promoted to the Championship.[[57]](#cite_note-57) [Hartlepool United](/wiki/Hartlepool_United_F.C. "Hartlepool United F.C.") and [Walsall](/wiki/Walsall_F.C. "Walsall F.C.") are both promoted to League One.[[58]](#cite_note-58)[[59]](#cite_note-59) [Rotherham United](/wiki/Rotherham_United_F.C. "Rotherham United F.C.") are relegated to League Two.[[60]](#cite_note-60)
* 20 April 2007 – [Luton Town](/wiki/Luton_Town_F.C. "Luton Town F.C.") are relegated to League One.[[61]](#cite_note-61)
* 21 April 2007 – [Watford](/wiki/Watford_F.C. "Watford F.C.") are relegated from the Premiership.[[62]](#cite_note-62) [Droylsden](/wiki/Droylsden_F.C. "Droylsden F.C.") are promoted to the Conference.[[63]](#cite_note-droylsden-63)
* 22 April 2007 – [Cristiano Ronaldo](/wiki/Cristiano_Ronaldo "Cristiano Ronaldo") is named [PFA Player of the Year](/wiki/PFA_Player_of_the_Year "PFA Player of the Year") as well as [PFA Young Player of the Year](/wiki/PFA_Young_Player_of_the_Year "PFA Young Player of the Year"). Eight out of the eleven players who are named in the PFA Premiership Team of the Year are Manchester United players, including Ronaldo.[[64]](#cite_note-64)
* 24 April 2007 – [Tamworth](/wiki/Tamworth_F.C. "Tamworth F.C.") and [Southport](/wiki/Southport_F.C. "Southport F.C.") are relegated from the Conference.[[65]](#cite_note-upsanddowns-65) Manchester United beat [Milan](/wiki/A.C._Milan "A.C. Milan") 3–2 in the Champions League semi-final first leg at Old Trafford.[[66]](#cite_note-66)
* 28 April 2007 – [Southend United](/wiki/Southend_United_F.C. "Southend United F.C.") are relegated from the Championship.[[67]](#cite_note-67) [Chesterfield](/wiki/Chesterfield_F.C. "Chesterfield F.C.") and [Bradford City](/wiki/Bradford_City_A.F.C. "Bradford City A.F.C.") are both relegated from League One.[[68]](#cite_note-68)
* 29 April 2007 – [Derby County](/wiki/Derby_County_F.C. "Derby County F.C.") lose to [Crystal Palace](/wiki/Crystal_Palace_F.C. "Crystal Palace F.C.") meaning that [Birmingham City](/wiki/Birmingham_City_F.C. "Birmingham City F.C.") and [Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland_A.F.C. "Sunderland A.F.C.") are promoted to the Premiership.[[69]](#cite_note-69) [Sam Allardyce](/wiki/Sam_Allardyce "Sam Allardyce") resigns after seven and a half years as manager of [Bolton Wanderers](/wiki/Bolton_Wanderers_F.C. "Bolton Wanderers F.C.").[[70]](#cite_note-70)
* 30 April 2007 – [Sammy Lee](/wiki/Sammy_Lee_(footballer) "Sammy Lee (footballer)") is promoted from the coaching staff to the manager's seat at Bolton Wanderers.[[71]](#cite_note-71)
* 30 April 2007 – Manchester United remain five points ahead of Chelsea with both left with three games to play, but both have secured automatic Champions League qualification, meaning Liverpool and Arsenal will both have to face a two-legged tie to secure qualification to the group stage. Two points separate Bolton, Everton, Reading, Portsmouth and Tottenham in the race to qualify for the [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup "UEFA Cup"). At the bottom of the table Watford have already been relegated, with Charlton and a resurgent West Ham completing the relegation zone but still with a slim chance of survival at the possible expense of Wigan, Fulham, Sheffield United and Middlesbrough.
* 1 May 2007 – [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C.") beat [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C.") on penalties in the Champions League semi-final at [Anfield](/wiki/Anfield "Anfield") after both sides won their home leg 1–0.[[72]](#cite_note-72)
* 2 May 2007 – Manchester United's hopes of an all-English European Cup final with Liverpool and second Treble are ended when they lose 3–0 to Milan in the semi-final second leg at the [San Siro](/wiki/San_Siro_(stadium) "San Siro (stadium)").[[73]](#cite_note-73)
* 4 May 2007 – [Leeds United](/wiki/Leeds_United_F.C. "Leeds United F.C.") are relegated to League One for the first time in their history after they go into [administration](/wiki/Administration_(insolvency) "Administration (insolvency)").[[74]](#cite_note-74)
* 5 May 2007 – [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") come out on top in the [Manchester derby](/wiki/Manchester_derby "Manchester derby") beating [Manchester City](/wiki/Manchester_City_F.C. "Manchester City F.C.") 1–0. In doing so United extend their lead at the top of the Premiership to 8 points. City's failure to score means that they set a new record for the fewest goals scored at home in a season by a club in the top flight, with only 10 scored in the season.[[75]](#cite_note-75) [Bristol City](/wiki/Bristol_City_F.C. "Bristol City F.C.") are promoted to the Championship.[[76]](#cite_note-news.bbc.co.uk-76) [Boston United](/wiki/Boston_United_F.C. "Boston United F.C.") are relegated to the Conference.[[76]](#cite_note-news.bbc.co.uk-76) [Swindon Town](/wiki/Swindon_Town_F.C. "Swindon Town F.C.") are promoted to League One and [Walsall](/wiki/Walsall_F.C. "Walsall F.C.") are League Two champions.[[77]](#cite_note-77) [MK Dons](/wiki/MK_Dons "MK Dons") moved out of the [National Hockey Stadium](/wiki/Former_England_National_Hockey_Stadium "Former England National Hockey Stadium") and into [stadium:mk](/wiki/Stadium:mk "Stadium:mk")[[78]](#cite_note-78)
* 6 May 2007 – [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") win the [Premiership](/wiki/2006%E2%80%9307_FA_Premier_League "2006–07 FA Premier League") after [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C.") only draw 1–1 at [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C.").[[79]](#cite_note-79) [Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland_A.F.C. "Sunderland A.F.C.") win the [Championship](/wiki/Football_League_Championship "Football League Championship") with a 5–0 win at [Luton Town](/wiki/Luton_Town_F.C. "Luton Town F.C."), whilst [Birmingham City](/wiki/Birmingham_City_F.C. "Birmingham City F.C.") lose 1–0 at [Preston North End](/wiki/Preston_North_End_F.C. "Preston North End F.C.").[[80]](#cite_note-80)
* 7 May 2007 – [Arsenal Ladies](/wiki/Arsenal_L.F.C. "Arsenal L.F.C.") completed an unprecedented Quadruple of trophies, defeating [Charlton Athletic](/wiki/Charlton_Athletic_L.F.C. "Charlton Athletic L.F.C.") 4–1 in the [FA Women's Cup](/wiki/FA_Women%27s_Cup "FA Women's Cup") final, to add to their [FA Women's Premier League](/wiki/FA_Women%27s_Premier_League "FA Women's Premier League"), [Women's League Cup](/wiki/Women%27s_League_Cup "Women's League Cup") and [UEFA Women's Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Women%27s_Cup "UEFA Women's Cup") victories.[[81]](#cite_note-81) [Charlton Athletic](/wiki/Charlton_Athletic_F.C. "Charlton Athletic F.C.") are relegated from the Premiership after they lose to [Tottenham Hotspur](/wiki/Tottenham_Hotspur_F.C. "Tottenham Hotspur F.C.").[[82]](#cite_note-82)
* 13 May 2007 – [Sheffield United](/wiki/Sheffield_United_F.C. "Sheffield United F.C.") are relegated from the Premiership after losing 2–1 at home to [Wigan Athletic](/wiki/Wigan_Athletic_F.C. "Wigan Athletic F.C.") and [West Ham United](/wiki/West_Ham_United_F.C. "West Ham United F.C.") beat [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") 1–0 at Old Trafford.[[83]](#cite_note-83) The season ends with Manchester United champions, Chelsea runners-up and Liverpool and Arsenal completing the top four. Tottenham, Everton and Bolton all qualify for the UEFA Cup.
* 16 May 2007 – [Neil Warnock](/wiki/Neil_Warnock "Neil Warnock") resigns after seven and a half years in charge of Sheffield United.[[84]](#cite_note-84)
* 18 May 2007 – [The Football League](/wiki/The_Football_League "The Football League") confirms that [Boston United](/wiki/Boston_United_F.C. "Boston United F.C.") were docked ten points for entering a Company Voluntary Arrangement in the final seconds of their [League Two](/wiki/Football_League_Two "Football League Two") defeat at [Wrexham](/wiki/Wrexham_A.F.C. "Wrexham A.F.C."), a game which saw *The Pilgrims* relegated.[[85]](#cite_note-85)
* 19 May 2007 – [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C.") beat [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") 1–0 to win the [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup").[[86]](#cite_note-86) at the first FA Cup Final at the new Wembley and stop Manchester United winning the double.
* 20 May 2007 – [Morecambe](/wiki/Morecambe_F.C. "Morecambe F.C.") are promoted to the Football League for the first time in their history after beating [Exeter City](/wiki/Exeter_City_F.C. "Exeter City F.C.") 2–1 in the Conference National play-off final.[[87]](#cite_note-Exeter_1-2_Morecambe-87)
* 23 May 2007 – A.C. Milan beat Liverpool 2–1 in the European Cup final in [Athens](/wiki/Athens "Athens"), two years after Liverpool had beaten the Italians on penalties after a 3–3 draw in the [Istanbul](/wiki/Istanbul "Istanbul") final.[[88]](#cite_note-88)
* 26 May 2007 – [Bristol Rovers](/wiki/Bristol_Rovers_F.C. "Bristol Rovers F.C.") win the League Two play-off final.[[89]](#cite_note-89)
* 27 May 2007 – [Blackpool](/wiki/Blackpool_F.C. "Blackpool F.C.") win the League One play off final.[[90]](#cite_note-90)
* 28 May 2007 – [Derby County](/wiki/Derby_County_F.C. "Derby County F.C.") defeat [West Bromwich Albion](/wiki/West_Bromwich_Albion_F.C. "West Bromwich Albion F.C.") 1–0 in the Championship play-off final.[[91]](#cite_note-91)
* 20 June 2007 – [Nottingham Forest](/wiki/Nottingham_Forest_F.C. "Nottingham Forest F.C.") announce plans to relocate from the [City Ground](/wiki/City_Ground "City Ground") to a new 50,000-seat stadium by 2014.[[92]](#cite_note-92) [Scarborough](/wiki/Scarborough_F.C. "Scarborough F.C."), who were relegated from the [Conference North](/wiki/Conference_North "Conference North") this season but are best remembered for being [Football League](/wiki/Football_League "Football League") members from 1897 to 1999, go out of business with debts of £2.5million.[[93]](#cite_note-93)
* 25 June 2007 – Five days after the demise of the 128-year-old Scarborough FC, a new club – [Scarborough Athletic](/wiki/Scarborough_Athletic_F.C. "Scarborough Athletic F.C.") – is formed.[[94]](#cite_note-94)

## League tables

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2006%E2%80%9307_in_English_football&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: League tables")]

### FA Premier League

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2006%E2%80%9307_in_English_football&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: FA Premier League")]

Main article: [2006–07 FA Premier League](/wiki/2006%E2%80%9307_FA_Premier_League "2006–07 FA Premier League")

After 3 seasons of missing out, [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") fought off the challenge of [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C.") and regained the Premier League title for the 9th time in 15 years. Despite finishing second, the Blues claimed a League Cup and FA Cup double, and Didier Drogba was the top flight's leading goalscorer with 20 in the league. The final two Champions League places went to [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C.") (who nearly won the competition for the 2nd time in 3 seasons only to lose out to [A.C. Milan](/wiki/A.C._Milan "A.C. Milan"), the same opponents from the final 2 years previous) and [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C."). [Tottenham](/wiki/Tottenham_Hotspur_F.C. "Tottenham Hotspur F.C.") and [Everton](/wiki/Everton_F.C. "Everton F.C.") qualified for the [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup "UEFA Cup"), as did [Bolton](/wiki/Bolton_Wanderers_F.C. "Bolton Wanderers F.C."), despite the departure of long-serving manager [Sam Allardyce](/wiki/Sam_Allardyce "Sam Allardyce") shortly before the end of the season. [Blackburn](/wiki/Blackburn_Rovers_F.C. "Blackburn Rovers F.C.") qualified for the [Intertoto Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Intertoto_Cup "UEFA Intertoto Cup") thanks to the 18 goals of striker [Benni McCarthy](/wiki/Benni_McCarthy "Benni McCarthy") as well as the impressive efforts of manager [Mark Hughes](/wiki/Mark_Hughes "Mark Hughes").

Newly promoted [Reading](/wiki/Reading_F.C. "Reading F.C."), tipped by many critics for relegation, defied the odds by finishing 8th on their first ever season in the top flight. [Portsmouth](/wiki/Portsmouth_F.C. "Portsmouth F.C.") put last season's managerial debacle behind them to finish 9th, finishing just 2 points short of European qualification. [Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa_F.C. "Aston Villa F.C.")'s campaign, their first under former Celtic manager [Martin O'Neill](/wiki/Martin_O%27Neill "Martin O'Neill"), marked an improvement on the previous campaign which saw them go undefeated in their first nine and last nine matches, although a staggering 17 draws prevented a top-half finish.

[Watford](/wiki/Watford_F.C. "Watford F.C.") finished bottom, managing only 5 wins all season as they made a swift return to the Championship. The loss of [Alan Curbishley](/wiki/Alan_Curbishley "Alan Curbishley") and 3 managerial changes in [Iain Dowie](/wiki/Iain_Dowie "Iain Dowie"), [Les Reed](/wiki/Les_Reed_(football_coach) "Les Reed (football coach)") and then [Alan Pardew](/wiki/Alan_Pardew "Alan Pardew") ended [Charlton Athletic](/wiki/Charlton_Athletic_F.C. "Charlton Athletic F.C.")'s 7-year stay in the top flight. Ironically, Curbishley took charge at [West Ham](/wiki/West_Ham_United_F.C. "West Ham United F.C."), another London club who looked certain for the drop after a poor season. However, a run of seven wins from their final nine matches was enough to secure their Premiership status, with a goal from [Carlos Tevez](/wiki/Carlos_Tevez "Carlos Tevez") giving the Hammers victory over champions Manchester United at Old Trafford, while sending [Sheffield United](/wiki/Sheffield_United_F.C. "Sheffield United F.C.") down and beginning speculation from the Yorkshire club over whether Tevez was eligible to play. The matter was eventually settled out of court, with West Ham fined £5.5 million by the Premier League and ordered to pay the Blades compensation over five years. [Wigan Athletic](/wiki/Wigan_Athletic_F.C. "Wigan Athletic F.C."), in their second year in the top flight, narrowly avoided relegation on goal difference.

Blackburn Rovers occupied the Intertoto place, because Portsmouth and Reading did not apply.[[194]](#cite_note-195) A further place in the UEFA Cup was up for grabs via the Premiership Fair Play League. The winner is placed into a draw with the winners of Fair Play leagues in other countries. The representatives from the two countries that come out of the hat first are given a place in the UEFA Cup first qualifying round. Since the winners of the Premiership Fair Play League, Tottenham Hotspur, had already qualified for the UEFA Cup by virtue of their league position, their place in the Fair Play draw was given to Aston Villa. However, the places in the UEFA Cup were awarded to the representatives from Finland and Norway.

### Football League Championship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2006%E2%80%9307_in_English_football&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Football League Championship")]

Main article: [2006–07 Football League Championship](/wiki/2006%E2%80%9307_Football_League_Championship "2006–07 Football League Championship")

After losing their first four games, [Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland_A.F.C. "Sunderland A.F.C.") looked ominous for a second relegation. The surprise appointment of [Roy Keane](/wiki/Roy_Keane "Roy Keane") by rookie chairman [Niall Quinn](/wiki/Niall_Quinn "Niall Quinn") paid off and they surged up the table, losing just one of their final 20 games to clinch promotion as champions. Keane's former Manchester United colleague, [Steve Bruce](/wiki/Steve_Bruce "Steve Bruce") also took [Birmingham City](/wiki/Birmingham_City_F.C. "Birmingham City F.C.") back into the Premier League, ensuring that they only remained in the Championship for one season.

[Derby County](/wiki/Derby_County_F.C. "Derby County F.C.") spent half the season in the top 2, but fell away in the final weeks to slip into the play-off places. Nonetheless, they won promotion by beating [West Bromwich Albion](/wiki/West_Bromwich_Albion_F.C. "West Bromwich Albion F.C.") 1–0 in the final at the recently opened new Wembley Stadium. This denied the Baggies an immediate return to the Premier League, which would have meant all 3 relegated clubs from the previous season were promoted.

[Preston](/wiki/Preston_North_End_F.C. "Preston North End F.C.") were perhaps the biggest chokers as they lost 5 of their final 7 games to slump out of the play-off places they had occupied through the bulk of the season, missing out on a third successive play-off finish. [Cardiff City](/wiki/Cardiff_City_F.C. "Cardiff City F.C.") had been the early pace-setters, leading the table up until the midway point before their form tailed off badly in the second half.

After a play-off final appearance the previous year, [Leeds](/wiki/Leeds_United_F.C. "Leeds United F.C.") finished bottom amidst yet more financial worries and acrimony, falling into the relegation zone in early October and, despite a late improvement, never leaving it. Their placing at the foot of the table was due to a 10-point deduction they suffered after going into voluntary administration after their relegation was all but confirmed on the penultimate weekend. The club came close to being expelled from the Football League during the summer after being unable to agree a deal with their creditors, but they were allowed to remain for the following season, albeit with a 15-point deduction. Regardless, it meant they would spend next season competing outside of the top two divisions for the first time in their history.

[Luton](/wiki/Luton_Town_F.C. "Luton Town F.C."), who would have finished bottom but for Leeds's deduction, were relegated after being forced to sell many of their top players due to mounting financial problems. They had spent the first half of the season safely in mid-table, but a disastrous run of form after the turn of the year saw them relegated back to League One after just two seasons. [Southend](/wiki/Southend_United_F.C. "Southend United F.C.") lasted only a single season in the Championship after their two successive promotions; their season was essentially the opposite of Luton's, winning just three games until the turn of the year before an improvement in form gave them some hope. [Colchester](/wiki/Colchester_United_F.C. "Colchester United F.C.") fared best of the newly promoted clubs, comfortably finishing in 10th, largely on the back of their strong home form at the division's smallest stadium, [Layer Road](/wiki/Layer_Road "Layer Road").

### Football League One

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2006%E2%80%9307_in_English_football&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Football League One")]

Main article: [2006–07 Football League § Football League One](/wiki/2006%E2%80%9307_Football_League#Football_League_One "2006–07 Football League")

With club physiotherapist [Nigel Adkins](/wiki/Nigel_Adkins "Nigel Adkins") promoted to the manager's role, [Scunthorpe United](/wiki/Scunthorpe_United_F.C. "Scunthorpe United F.C.") topped the league as champions, having been in the bottom two divisions since the early 1960s. Prolific striker [Billy Sharp](/wiki/Billy_Sharp "Billy Sharp") was the also the division's leading marksman with 30 goals. [Gary Johnson](/wiki/Gary_Johnson "Gary Johnson") helped [Bristol City](/wiki/Bristol_City_F.C. "Bristol City F.C.") achieve automatic-promotion after nearly a decade of near-misses and 2 play-off defeats, ending their eight-year exile from the Championship. [Blackpool](/wiki/Blackpool_F.C. "Blackpool F.C.") won the play-offs, ending nearly three decades in the bottom two divisions.

[Brentford](/wiki/Brentford_F.C. "Brentford F.C."), who lost their manager [Martin Allen](/wiki/Martin_Allen "Martin Allen") just before the start of the season, finished bottom, having a dismal run of 21 games without a win. [Rotherham](/wiki/Rotherham_United_F.C. "Rotherham United F.C.") won their first few games, wiping out their ten-point deduction early in the season; they ended up finishing thirteen points adrift however, and were relegated. Having started the decade in the Premier League, [Bradford City](/wiki/Bradford_City_A.F.C. "Bradford City A.F.C.") fell into the bottom division for the first time in twenty-five years, with [Chesterfield](/wiki/Chesterfield_F.C. "Chesterfield F.C.") occupying the final relegation spot.

### Football League Two

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2006%E2%80%9307_in_English_football&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Football League Two")]

Main article: [2006–07 Football League § Football League Two](/wiki/2006%E2%80%9307_Football_League#Football_League_Two "2006–07 Football League")

The four teams relegated from League One in 2005–06 would occupy the top four this season, sending [Walsall](/wiki/Walsall_F.C. "Walsall F.C."), [Hartlepool United](/wiki/Hartlepool_United_F.C. "Hartlepool United F.C.") and [Swindon Town](/wiki/Swindon_Town_F.C. "Swindon Town F.C.") back up. [Bristol Rovers](/wiki/Bristol_Rovers_F.C. "Bristol Rovers F.C.") won the play-offs however, returning to League One after six years.

[Torquay United](/wiki/Torquay_United_F.C. "Torquay United F.C.") had been both the last team to finish bottom under the old election system, and the last team to finish bottom of the League and survive due to the Conference champions not having a good enough ground. However, this season they finished bottom and dropped out of the League. They were joined by [Boston United](/wiki/Boston_United_F.C. "Boston United F.C."), who had voluntary arrangements in the 87th minute of the season's final game, but would still have been relegated even without the 10-point administration penalty. In a first since the introduction of automatic promotion and relegation between the Football League and Conference, Boston were relegated two divisions due to failing to pay footballing creditors, along with financial irregularities committed during their promotion season in 2001–02.

## Transfer deals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2006%E2%80%9307_in_English_football&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Transfer deals")]

Main article: [List of English football transfers 2006–07](/wiki/List_of_English_football_transfers_2006%E2%80%9307 "List of English football transfers 2006–07")

The summer transfer window saw many high-profile moves. These included [Andriy Shevchenko](/wiki/Andriy_Shevchenko "Andriy Shevchenko") and [Michael Ballack](/wiki/Michael_Ballack "Michael Ballack") joining [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C."),[[227]](#cite_note-234)[[228]](#cite_note-235) and [Ruud van Nistelrooy](/wiki/Ruud_van_Nistelrooy "Ruud van Nistelrooy") leaving [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") to join [Real Madrid](/wiki/Real_Madrid "Real Madrid").[[229]](#cite_note-236) [West Ham United](/wiki/West_Ham_United_F.C. "West Ham United F.C.") secured the surprise double signing of [Javier Mascherano](/wiki/Javier_Mascherano "Javier Mascherano") and [Carlos Tevez](/wiki/Carlos_Tevez "Carlos Tevez") from [Corinthians](/wiki/Sport_Club_Corinthians_Paulista "Sport Club Corinthians Paulista"),[[230]](#cite_note-237) and [Dietmar Hamann](/wiki/Dietmar_Hamann "Dietmar Hamann")'s transfer to [Bolton Wanderers](/wiki/Bolton_Wanderers_F.C. "Bolton Wanderers F.C.") became the shortest in English footballing history.[[231]](#cite_note-238)

The January transfer window was quieter than the summer, with [Ashley Young](/wiki/Ashley_Young "Ashley Young")'s £9.65m move to [Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa_F.C. "Aston Villa F.C.")[[232]](#cite_note-239) and [Matthew Upson](/wiki/Matthew_Upson "Matthew Upson")'s £6m move to [West Ham United](/wiki/West_Ham_United_F.C. "West Ham United F.C.")[[233]](#cite_note-240) the window's most expensive.

In total, Premiership clubs spent the highest amount on transfers in the summer since the transfer window system was introduced.[[234]](#cite_note-241)

## Deaths

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2006%E2%80%9307_in_English_football&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Deaths")]

* 18 July 2006 – **[Jimmy Leadbetter](/wiki/Jimmy_Leadbetter "Jimmy Leadbetter")**, 78, former [Ipswich Town](/wiki/Ipswich_Town_F.C. "Ipswich Town F.C.") winger.[[258]](#cite_note-265)
* 21 July 2006 – **[Bert Slater](/wiki/Bert_Slater "Bert Slater")**, 70, former [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C.") and [Watford](/wiki/Watford_F.C. "Watford F.C.") goalkeeper.[[259]](#cite_note-266)
* 28 July 2006 – **[Sep Smith](/wiki/Sep_Smith "Sep Smith")**, 94, former [Leicester City](/wiki/Leicester_City_F.C. "Leicester City F.C.") wing-half, and oldest living [England](/wiki/England_national_football_team "England national football team") international.[[260]](#cite_note-267)
* 28 July 2006 – **[Billy Walsh](/wiki/Billy_Walsh_(Irish_footballer) "Billy Walsh (Irish footballer)")**, 85, former [Manchester City](/wiki/Manchester_City_F.C. "Manchester City F.C.") defender, and [Republic of Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland_national_football_team "Republic of Ireland national football team") international.[[261]](#cite_note-268)
* 18 August 2006 – **[Norman Rowlinson](/wiki/Norman_Rowlinson "Norman Rowlinson")**, 83, former chairman of [Crewe Alexandra](/wiki/Crewe_Alexandra_F.C. "Crewe Alexandra F.C.").[[262]](#cite_note-269)
* 2 September 2006 – **[Lionel Pickering](/wiki/Lionel_Pickering "Lionel Pickering")**, 74, former chairman of [Derby County](/wiki/Derby_County_F.C. "Derby County F.C.") and noted businessman. Bought the club from [Robert Maxwell](/wiki/Robert_Maxwell "Robert Maxwell") in 1990 and owned it for 13 years, during which time Derby spent six seasons in the [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League") and moved into a [new stadium](/wiki/Pride_Park_Stadium "Pride Park Stadium").[[263]](#cite_note-270)
* 2 September 2006 – **[Charlie Williams](/wiki/Charlie_Williams_(comedian) "Charlie Williams (comedian)")**, 77, former [Doncaster Rovers](/wiki/Doncaster_Rovers_F.C. "Doncaster Rovers F.C.") defender who later became a successful comedian.[[264]](#cite_note-271)

187. **[^](#cite_ref-187)** [*UEFA decision on Israel*](http://www.uefa.com/uefa/Keytopics/kind=64/newsId=442693.html) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20071013175135/http://www.uefa.com/uefa/Keytopics/kind%3D64/newsId%3D442693.html) 13 October 2007 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine"), *[UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA")*, 7 August 2006.  
     Due to the [then ongoing Israel-Lebanon conflict](/wiki/2006_Israel-Lebanon_conflict "2006 Israel-Lebanon conflict"), UEFA ruled that no matches in competitions it controls could be held in Israel until further notice.
188. **[^](#cite_ref-188)** [*UEFA lifts Israel match ban*](http://www.uefa.com/uefa/Keytopics/kind=64/newsId=457461.html) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20070107120947/http://www.uefa.com/uefa/keytopics/kind%3D64/newsid%3D457461.html) 7 January 2007 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine"), *UEFA*, 15 September 2006.  
     UEFA later lifted its ban on matches in Israel after a cease-fire in the conflict. Matches may only be played in the [Tel Aviv](/wiki/Tel_Aviv "Tel Aviv") area, which includes the Ramat Gan stadium in Ramat Gan.
